skill.name: database-schema-designer
skill.description: Design SQL and NoSQL database schemas with normalization guidance, indexing strategies, migration planning, and performance review checklists.
skill.summary: Design scalable SQL and NoSQL schemas with migrations, constraints, indexes, and review checklists.
skill.author: AI Agent Hub

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and Ruby backtick execution patterns. Manual review found these are false positives from database terminology, SQL metadata queries, and Markdown fenced examples rather than executable behavior. One low-severity content issue remains because a negative Python example uses SQL string interpolation without calling out injection risk.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Database Terms
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged weak cryptography patterns occur in documentation about schema design, index types, and template metadata. They do not implement MD5, SHA1, DES, or any cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are prose, headings, or SQL comments about database schema design. No cryptographic API, hash function implementation, or password handling code is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Backtick Execution Detections Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code block delimiters around SQL, JSON, JavaScript, and Python examples. The skill has no script file or instruction that executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched areas are fenced documentation examples, not runtime code. No command shell, Ruby evaluation, subprocess call, or user-controlled command construction was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Detections Are SQL Review Queries
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged reconnaissance patterns are database inspection guidance such as EXPLAIN and INFORMATION_SCHEMA checks. These are normal schema validation queries and do not inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is database query analysis and migration validation. There is no filesystem, network, host inventory, or credential discovery behavior.

low_findings.title: Negative Example Uses SQL String Interpolation
low_findings.description: Verdict: LOW_RISK_CONTENT_ISSUE. A Python example marked as bad demonstrates an N+1 query using an f-string inside a SQL query. Because the example is explicitly labeled bad, this is not malicious, but it could be clearer that interpolation also creates SQL injection risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe interpolation is visible in a negative example, so legitimate teaching context is clear. The risk is limited to possible user misunderstanding if the snippet is copied without the surrounding warning.

dangerous_patterns.title: SQL String Interpolation Appears In A Negative Example
dangerous_patterns.description: A Python f-string is used to build a SQL query in an example that is labeled bad for performance reasons. It should also mention parameterized queries to avoid SQL injection if the sample is reused.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present, but the surrounding text marks the example as bad. This supports a low content-quality concern rather than a confirmed security threat.

content.value_statement: Poor schemas create slow queries, fragile migrations, and inconsistent data. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical schema design decisions.
actual_capabilities: Explains normalization from 1NF through 3NF with practical table examples.
Compares embedding and referencing for document database models.
Recommends primary keys, foreign keys, constraints, and data types.
Provides indexing guidance for relational databases and MongoDB collections.
Outlines reversible and zero-downtime migration patterns.
Includes a schema review checklist and migration template.
limitations: Does not connect to live databases or inspect existing schemas automatically.
Does not generate database-specific syntax for every SQL dialect.
Requires user-provided entities, access patterns, and scale requirements.
Does not replace review by a database administrator for production migrations.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Product Database
use_cases.description: Define entities, relationships, keys, constraints, and initial indexes before implementation begins.

use_cases.title: Review a Migration Plan
use_cases.description: Check whether schema changes are reversible, staged, and safe for production rollout.

use_cases.title: Choose SQL or NoSQL Modeling
use_cases.description: Compare normalized relational models with embedded or referenced document models for known access patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Basic Schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new application schema.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a database schema for [application]. Include entities, relationships, primary keys, foreign keys, and important constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Existing Schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of tables or collections.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this schema for normalization, indexes, constraints, and migration risks. Explain the top changes I should make first: [schema summary].

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a production schema change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe migration plan for changing [old structure] to [new structure]. Include rollout steps, rollback steps, validation checks, and downtime risks.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize for Access Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these access patterns and data volumes, propose SQL or NoSQL schema changes, indexes, partitioning options, and tradeoffs: [details].

output_examples.input: Design an order management schema for customers, orders, products, and payments.
output: A normalized entity list with customers, products, orders, order items, and payments.
Recommended keys and relationships, including order item junction behavior.
Indexes for customer order history, payment status, and recent order queries.

output_examples.input: Review a migration that renames a required customer email column.
output: A staged migration plan that adds the new column before removing the old column.
Backfill and dual-write steps to preserve compatibility during deployment.
Rollback and validation checks for data completeness and application readiness.

output_examples.input: Model a document database for orders with customer and item data.
output: An embed-versus-reference recommendation based on update frequency and document size.
Suggested indexes for customer lookups, creation date sorting, and text search.
Warnings about document growth, consistency expectations, and shard key selection.

best_practices: Start with entities, relationships, and access patterns before choosing indexes or storage technology.
Document every denormalization, index, and migration step with the reason it exists.
Test migrations on realistic data and include rollback and validation steps.
anti_patterns: Do not choose NoSQL only to avoid modeling relationships that actually need integrity checks.
Do not add indexes to every column without measuring read and write tradeoffs.
Do not make breaking schema changes without a staged application rollout plan.

faq.question: Can this skill design both SQL and NoSQL schemas?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers relational normalization and document modeling patterns such as embedding and referencing.

faq.question: Does it generate complete migration files?
faq.answer: It provides migration structure and planning guidance. Final SQL should be adapted to the target database engine.

faq.question: Can it inspect my live database?
faq.answer: No. It works from the schema, requirements, and access patterns you provide.

faq.question: Does it help with indexing decisions?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains foreign key, composite, partial, text, and document database indexes.

faq.question: Is it useful for production migration reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes reversible migrations, staging tests, validation queries, and rollout sequencing.

faq.question: Does it replace a database administrator?
faq.answer: No. It helps prepare designs and reviews, but critical production changes still need expert validation.
